preFix.title: Stone Pattern Marbling
preFix.subtitle: Dropping colour to build concentric rings on the size surface
preFix.excerpt: The stone pattern is the foundational marbled design: colour dropped onto size, each drop pushing the last outward into concentric rings. It is valid as a finished design in its own right and forms the starting point for every combed pattern. Understanding how drop size, sequence, and spacing affect the result gives control over both uses.
preFix.sourceNotes: Halfer, Josef. Progress of the Marbling Art. English translation. Buffalo, NY: O.H. Neumann, 1893/1894.

attrs.body: Alum is a mild skin and eye irritant. Wear gloves when mixing or applying the mordant and rinse splashes promptly. Carrageenan is non-toxic but spilled size is slippery; wipe spills immediately. Do not eat or drink at the marbling tray.
attrs.heading: Marbling chemistry safety

content.text: The mechanics of the stone
Every
pigment drop

that lands on the carrageenan surface spreads outward until its surface tension equalises with that of the surrounding size. When a second drop of a different colour lands nearby, it spreads too, and its spreading edge pushes the boundary of the first drop further outward. The first drop becomes an outer ring; the second is now the centre.
A third drop on the same spot pushes the second outward, which in turn pushes the first further still. The result is the
stone pattern
: a concentric ring structure, like the cross-section of a tree, with the most recently dropped colour at the centre and the earliest at the outer edge.
Preparing the mordant
The
mordant
makes the paint stick to the paper rather than remaining on the size when the paper is lifted. Dissolve potash alum at 25 to 30 g per litre of warm water. Allow to cool before applying.

Apply the alum solution to the paper using a sponge or foam brush. Work evenly across the whole surface. Allow to dry completely before printing. Pre-mordanted alum paper is also available commercially for beginners who want to skip this step initially.
Preparing the colours

Marbling acrylic paint must be thinned with water and a surfactant (typically ox gall) so that it floats rather than sinks. The correct thin is a matter of testing: fill a small jar with size, add a drop of the prepared colour, and observe. It should spread steadily to a diameter of 3 to 6 cm. If it sinks, add more surfactant or thin further. If it spreads more than 10 cm, the paint is too thin and will not have enough colour density to print.

Prepare each colour in a small dropper bottle or in a cup with a stylus (a pointed stick or brush handle works). Keep the colours covered when not in use; surface-dried paint in the dropper will block it.
Dropping the pattern

Skim the size surface to remove bubbles before beginning. Hold the dropper or stylus tip 5 to 8 cm above the surface. Let the drop fall; do not press or squeeze the dropper quickly. A gentle, gravity-fed drop produces an even circle. A squeezed drop creates a splash that fragments the nearby pattern.

For a simple stone with maximum concentric rings: drop colour one at a spot; drop colour two onto the same spot (it will push colour one outward); drop colour one again onto the centre; drop colour two again. Repeat the alternation. This creates a tightly nested set of rings from two colours at each drop point.

For a field of stones across the whole tray: work systematically in a grid. Drop colour one at each point in a grid, leaving about 6 cm between points. Then drop colour two at the same points. Then colour three. Each subsequent colour is added within the rings of the previous, adding a new innermost ring. Four to six colours produce a rich result.
The stone as a finished result

The stone pattern is often described as a preparatory stage for combed patterns, but it is a complete and historically valid marbled design. Halfer illustrates stone patterns as finished endpapers in the 1893 edition. A field of well-spaced, evenly sized stones in three or four colours is an elegant result.

The visual character of the stone is affected by the number of colours: two colours produce a graphic, high-contrast result; four or five produce a softer, more detailed ring structure. Very large drops create bold, widely spaced rings; many small drops create a denser, more intricate surface.
The stone as raw material for combed patterns

A stone-covered tray is the correct starting point for any combed pattern. The raking tools reorganise the stone rings into waves, chevrons, or feathers, but only have something to reorganise if the stone pattern covers the tray evenly. A sparse stone will produce a combed pattern with visible gaps.

For combed work, the stone drops should be smaller and more densely placed than for a stone displayed as finished work. The target is a surface with no bare size showing between ring structures, but without so much overlap that colours merge into an undifferentiated mass. This balance is the most important skill in stone-dropping, and it is learned through observation rather than rule.
Printing and rinsing

Lower the alum-treated paper face-down onto the size, releasing from one edge and rolling it flat to avoid air pockets. Leave for 30 to 45 seconds. Lift from one corner and carry to a rinse tray or gentle running water. Rinse to remove the size and excess paint from the back of the print. Hang or pin to dry.
